lesson.title: Functions
lesson.summary: Write reusable blocks of code with functions.

sections.heading: Defining a function
sections.bodyHtml:
A function is a reusable block of code. You give it a name and call it by that name.

func greet(name string) {
    fmt.Println("Hello,", name)
}

name string is a parameter — data you pass in.

Call it:

greet("Alice")
greet("Bob")

You have been using functions already: fmt.Println is a function.

sections.heading: Return values
sections.bodyHtml:
A function can send a value back with return.

func add(a, b int) int {
    return a + b
}

The int before { is the return type.

Use the result:

sum := add(3, 7)
fmt.Println(sum)   // 10

Go functions can return multiple values:

func divide(a, b int) (int, int) {
    return a / b, a % b
}

Call with two variables:

quotient, remainder := divide(10, 3)

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml: Open practice/functions/main.go. Write a function double(n int) int that returns n * 2. Then call it with a few numbers and print the results.

questions.prompt: How do you define a function named double that takes an int and returns an int?
What does return do inside a function?

options.label: Sends a value back to the caller and exits
Prints a value to the screen

questions.prompt: Can a Go function return multiple values?

options.label: Yes, by listing types in parentheses
No, Go only allows one return value
Only if you use a slice

exercises.title: Function calls
exercises.instructions: Run the program that uses double and greet functions.

exercises.title: Factorial
exercises.instructions: Run the recursive factorial function and confirm the outputs.
